item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, it's cute.
Not every Skip has to be a Universe ending format screw, some can be a pompous Renaissance Siri tragically unaware of what an anachronism it is.
It's almost a pet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Possibly. I intended the connection to be ambiguous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Am I the only one who noticed…

a religious organization claiming that SCP-1852 was "a piece of their god"

So.. even though the tag isn't there, this is a Church of the Broken God article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, on reading this again, I do find it kind of fun. Happy face.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In this particular case though, the SCP has flat out confirmed that it is a separate entity, and that the box is a communication medium. Also, while I'm just guessing, I would imagine the fact that the pieces can be individually replaced means that the functions of it are easily understood to be telecommunicative, which also supports the idea of a separate entity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That'd apply to all manner of SCPs though. The foundation could probably carve an identical 173 or soapstone statue, which wouldn't display any anomalous activity, but it'd be a stretch to then go on and say "173 is therefore two entities; the statue and an interdimensional intelligence that inhabits it". I'm somewhat sure I remember some other SCPs that can have their parts replaced and such but I can't remember which ones.

It just seems a rather arbitrary thing to declare for this one, somehow, especially when 1852-1 itself can't be directly observed in any way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We've built several replicas to the exact specifications of the box (which we know, since it can be disassembled and its parts replaced to the point that it's a Ship of Theseus), and none of them have powered up or produced statements from Dictus Ultima.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I might be missing something obvious here, but if the only way 1852-1 can be observed is through its interactions with 1852-2… are we even sure there IS an 1852-1? Why isn't it just the calculator box itself? I can't see anything in the article that implies there's an entity communicating through the device rather than it just being the device itself, other than the blunt statement that there just is, which isn't backed up anywhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It could have been 682; I just didn't feel like copying the blackboxes. Not quite sure what you mean by the second comment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Realistically none of the words would be capitalized since Morse code only uses one case. I capitalized it for ease of reading - it can be assumed from context that "Electricians" refers to a group or organization rather than simple electrical engineers, so it's capitalized.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The object in question is not 682, and the doctor wasn't expecting any useful information in response - he merely wanted to observe the entity's reaction.

As for the speed of the conversation, it did deliver a 17-page monologue in the form of Morse code.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Communication with this thing seems a bit too painfully slow. I'd speed it up a bit, if only so that I don't have to feel so sorry for the interviewers assigned to this thing. I found the question about destroying 682 to be kinda shoehorned in there. It's a pretty abrupt change from the previous line of questioning.

Also, I love this line:

Dr. Samesh: Is that a no?
SCP-1852-1: No is another way of saying yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do so love how it completely denied that man had done any of those things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh… it feels like it's been done, even though I can't say where. How did they know to capitalize Electricians in the interview?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, at least it's more cooperative than Jean-Philippe. And a lot less messy, too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this thing preaching the virtues of mastering steam power and industrialisation. It is now the Mr. Burns Box in my headcanon.

"Bah! I will not provide information to any organization that does not yet make wider use of the autogyro!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the implications this thing gives. The device itself is a parroting idiot, but whoever directed it's creation obviously knew something we don't. And if whatever eldritch, techno-gods it refers to are still alive then they're obviously close to making a move.

All in all it's a better execution of the idea than Prometheus. /Ridleyscottburn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are 4 black boxes, so whatever the interviewer was inquiring about, it wasn't 682. Serves the same purpose, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd love a slightly more final ending. I see the fun of asking about destroying SCP-682, but really we know full well that this thing is pretty stupid-why do we think it could tell us? Or give us an answer worth taking seriously?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's stated in the article that the object emits alpha radiation, so I didn't feel it was necessary to go into any further detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A pompous entity which attempts to give the appearance of being profound. I like it.

wear appropriate radiation shielding during testing

Isn't it the job of the containment procedure section to say what is appropriate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your collapsible is borked. Otherwise I enjoy, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New SCP based on an old Bright's challenge (specifically, turning this into an SCP).
